lessons.title: Chemical Equilibrium and Le Chatelier's Principle

content.body:
Reversible reaction: A + B ⇌ C + D
Equilibrium: forward rate = reverse rate
Concentrations constant (not equal) at equilibrium

Equilibrium constant Kc:
For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD:
Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b

Le Chatelier's Principle:
If system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to OPPOSE the change

content.title: Le Chatelier Applications
content.body:
N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃   ΔH = -92 kJ/mol

↑ pressure: shifts right (fewer moles of gas)
↓ pressure: shifts left (more moles of gas)
↑ temperature: shifts left (endothermic direction opposes heat increase)
↓ temperature: shifts right (exothermic direction, more NH₃)
Add catalyst: NO shift (equilibrium position unchanged, just reaches it faster)

content.title: Haber Process
content.body:
Industrial production of ammonia:
N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g)   ΔH = -92 kJ/mol
Conditions: 450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst
Compromise: lower T gives more NH₃ but slowly
Higher P gives more NH₃ but costly/dangerous
450°C balances yield and rate

questions.question: At chemical equilibrium
questions.explanation: Equilibrium: both reactions ongoing but at equal rates — concentrations remain constant

questions.question: Kc = [products]/[reactants]. Large Kc means
questions.explanation: Large Kc: numerator (products) >> denominator. Products are favoured at equilibrium

questions.question: Le Chatelier's Principle: increasing pressure shifts equilibrium toward
options: the side with more gas moles
the side with fewer gas moles
questions.answer: the side with fewer gas moles
questions.explanation: Higher pressure favours the side with fewer gas molecules (reduces volume to oppose pressure increase)

questions.question: For N₂+3H₂⇌2NH₃. Increasing temperature (exothermic reaction) will
options: decrease NH₃ yield (shifts left, endothermic direction
questions.answer: decrease NH₃ yield (shifts left, endothermic direction
questions.explanation: Endothermic direction absorbs the added heat — shifts backward, reducing product yield

questions.question: Adding a catalyst to a reaction at equilibrium
options: has no effect on equilibrium position — only speeds up reaching equilibrium
questions.answer: has no effect on equilibrium position — only speeds up reaching equilibrium
questions.explanation: Catalyst speeds both forward and reverse equally — same equilibrium position reached faster

questions.question: Haber process temperature compromise (450°C) reflects
options: catalyst requires this temperature; higher T reduces yield but lower T is too slow
questions.answer: catalyst requires this temperature; higher T reduces yield but lower T is too slow
questions.explanation: 450°C: Fe catalyst active, acceptable rate. Higher T: better rate but lower NH₃ yield (equilibrium shifts left)

questions.question: A(g) + 2B(g) ⇌ C(g). Kc = [C]/([A][B]²). Units of Kc =
questions.explanation: [C]/([A][B]²) = (mol/L) / (mol/L × (mol/L)²) = 1/(mol/L)² = L²/mol²

questions.question: Contact process for H₂SO₄: 2SO₂+O₂⇌2SO₃. Increasing O₂ concentration will
questions.explanation: Adding O₂: more reactant → equilibrium shifts right to use extra O₂, producing more SO₃

questions.question: Removing product from equilibrium mixture causes
options: equilibrium to shift right to replace removed product
questions.answer: equilibrium to shift right to replace removed product
questions.explanation: Removing product disturbs equilibrium; system responds by forming more product (shifts right)

questions.question: Which is NOT affected by a change in temperature at equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Temperature changes: rate (both reactions), position (Le Chatelier), AND Kc (only T changes Kc)

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Bronsted-Lowry: acid = H⁺ donor, base = H⁺ acceptor

Water autoionisation:
H₂O ⇌ H⁺ + OH⁻
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1×10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C
pH = -log[H⁺]
[H⁺] = 10^(-pH)
pOH = -log[OH⁻]
pH + pOH = 14

Strong acid: fully dissociates
[H⁺] = concentration of acid

Weak acid: Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]
pKa = -log Ka; smaller pKa = stronger acid

content.title: pH Calculations
content.body:
Strong acid: 0.01M HCl
[H⁺] = 0.01 mol/L
pH = -log(0.01) = 2

Strong base: 0.001M NaOH
[OH⁻] = 0.001
pOH = 3
pH = 14 - 3 = 11

Weak acid: Ka=4×10⁻⁵ for 0.1M ethanoic acid
[H⁺] = √(Ka×C) = √(4×10⁻⁵×0.1) = √(4×10⁻⁶) = 2×10⁻³
pH = -log(2×10⁻³) = 2.70

content.title: Buffer Solutions
content.body:
Buffer: resists pH change when small amounts of acid or alkali added
Weak acid + its conjugate base (e.g. CH₃COOH + CH₃COO⁻)
Adding H⁺: reacts with CH₃COO⁻
Adding OH⁻: reacts with CH₃COOH
pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA])  (Henderson-Hasselbalch)

questions.question: Bronsted-Lowry acid is
questions.explanation: Bronsted-Lowry: acid donates H⁺ (proton donor), base accepts H⁺

questions.question: pH of 0.001M HCl =
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1×10⁻¹⁴. Pure water [H⁺] =
questions.explanation: In pure water [H⁺]=[OH⁻]=√(1×10⁻¹⁴)=1×10⁻⁷, pH=7

questions.question: 0.1M NaOH has pH =
Weak acid has Ka=1×10⁻⁵. pKa =
Stronger acid has
questions.explanation: More acidic = larger Ka (more dissociation) = smaller pKa. Smaller pKa = stronger acid

questions.question: Buffer solution resists pH change because
options: it contains weak acid/conjugate base pair to neutralise added H⁺ or OH⁻
questions.answer: it contains weak acid/conjugate base pair to neutralise added H⁺ or OH⁻
questions.explanation: Buffer: weak acid neutralises added OH⁻; conjugate base neutralises added H⁺

questions.question: Blood is buffered at pH≈7.4. Main buffer system is
questions.explanation: Blood buffering: CO₂+H₂O⇌H₂CO₃⇌H⁺+HCO₃⁻; pH controlled by lungs (CO₂) and kidneys (HCO₃⁻)

questions.question: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). Equal concentrations: pH =
questions.explanation: If [A⁻]=[HA], log(1)=0, so pH=pKa

questions.question: A solution has [OH⁻]=2×10⁻³. [H⁺] =
What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Sparingly soluble salts:
AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq)
Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻]

For MₐXᵦ: Ksp = [M^(b+)]^a × [X^(a-)]^b

Solubility s:
AgCl: Ksp=[s][s]=s²  →  s=√Ksp
Ag₂CrO₄: Ksp=[2s]²[s]=4s³  →  s=(Ksp/4)^(1/3)

Common ion effect: adding common ion reduces solubility

content.title: Ksp Calculations
content.body:
AgCl: Ksp=1.8×10⁻¹⁰
s=√(1.8×10⁻¹⁰)=1.34×10⁻⁵ mol/L

PbSO₄: Ksp=1.6×10⁻⁸
PbSO₄⇌Pb²⁺+SO₄²⁻: Ksp=s²
s=√(1.6×10⁻⁸)=1.26×10⁻⁴ mol/L

Common ion: dissolve AgCl in 0.1M NaCl
[Cl⁻]=0.1+s≈0.1
Ksp=[Ag⁺][0.1]=1.8×10⁻¹⁰
[Ag⁺]=1.8×10⁻⁹ mol/L (much less than 1.34×10⁻⁵)

content.title: Precipitation
content.body:
Predict precipitation: if [M][X] > Ksp → precipitate forms
Ion product Q > Ksp → precipitate
Q < Ksp → solution unsaturated
Q = Ksp → saturated, at equilibrium

questions.question: Ksp is defined for
options: sparingly soluble ionic compounds in equilibrium with their ions
questions.answer: sparingly soluble ionic compounds in equilibrium with their ions
questions.explanation: Ksp (solubility product): equilibrium constant for dissolution of sparingly soluble ionic compounds

questions.question: For AgCl ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻, Ksp=1.8×10⁻¹⁰. Solubility s =
Common ion effect: adding NaCl to AgCl solution
options: decreases AgCl solubility (more Cl⁻ shifts equilibrium left)
questions.answer: decreases AgCl solubility (more Cl⁻ shifts equilibrium left)
questions.explanation: Extra Cl⁻ from NaCl → Le Chatelier: equilibrium shifts left → less AgCl dissolves

questions.question: Precipitation occurs when ion product Q is
questions.explanation: Q > Ksp: solution supersaturated → precipitate forms until Q = Ksp

questions.question: Ag₂CrO₄: Ksp=1.2×10⁻¹². Ksp=[Ag⁺]²[CrO₄²⁻]=[2s]²[s]=4s³. Solubility s=
Ksp of AgCl is larger than AgBr which is larger than AgI. This means
options: AgCl most soluble (largest Ksp=most ions in solution)
questions.answer: AgCl most soluble (largest Ksp=most ions in solution)
questions.explanation: Larger Ksp → more dissolved ions → greater solubility. AgCl > AgBr > AgI in solubility

questions.question: Selective precipitation separates ions by
options: adding reagent that precipitates one ion but not others based on different Ksp values
questions.answer: adding reagent that precipitates one ion but not others based on different Ksp values
questions.explanation: Add Cl⁻: AgCl precipitates (low Ksp) while NaCl stays dissolved → separate Ag⁺ from Na⁺

questions.question: Does BaSO₄ precipitate when 0.001M Ba²⁺ mixed with 0.001M SO₄²⁻? Ksp(BaSO₄)=1.1×10⁻¹⁰
questions.explanation: Q=[0.001][0.001]=10⁻⁶ >> 1.1×10⁻¹⁰=Ksp → precipitate forms

questions.question: Why is Ksp not affected by common ion effect?
options: Ksp is only a function of temperature — equilibrium constant unchanged
solubility and Ksp are the same
questions.answer: Ksp is only a function of temperature — equilibrium constant unchanged
questions.explanation: Ksp is an equilibrium constant — depends only on temperature. Solubility (not Ksp) decreases with common ion

questions.question: Hard water is caused by dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. Adding Na₂CO₃ (washing soda) softens water because
options: CO₃²⁻ reacts with Ca²⁺ → CaCO₃↓ precipitates removing Ca²⁺
questions.answer: CO₃²⁻ reacts with Ca²⁺ → CaCO₃↓ precipitates removing Ca²⁺
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ + CO₃²⁻ → CaCO₃↓ (Ksp very small). Removing Ca²⁺ softens the water

lessons.title: Electrochemical Cells and Standard Electrode Potentials

content.body:
Standard electrode potential E° (vs SHE at 25°C, 1M, 1atm):
More positive E°: stronger oxidising agent
More negative E°: stronger reducing agent

Cell potential: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode
Positive E°cell → spontaneous reaction

Nernst equation:
E = E° - (RT/nF)lnQ = E° - (0.0257/n)lnQ at 25°C
n=moles electrons, F=96485 C/mol

ΔG = -nFE°cell (+ linking to thermodynamics)

content.title: Cell Potential Examples
content.body:
Zn/Cu cell:
Zn²⁺/Zn: E°=-0.76V (anode, oxidation)
Cu²⁺/Cu: E°=+0.34V (cathode, reduction)
E°cell=0.34-(-0.76)=+1.10V (spontaneous)

Overall: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu

ΔG=-nFE°=-2×96485×1.10=-212,267 J=-212.3 kJ

content.title: Batteries and Fuel Cells
content.body:
Daniell cell: Zn/ZnSO₄||CuSO₄/Cu, 1.1V
Lead-acid (car): Pb/H₂SO₄/PbO₂, 2V per cell, 12V for 6 cells
Alkaline: Zn/MnO₂, 1.5V
Lithium-ion: 3.6V per cell, rechargeable
Hydrogen fuel cell: H₂+½O₂→H₂O, 1.23V, only water produced

questions.question: Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) is assigned E° =
questions.explanation: SHE: H⁺(1M)/H₂(1atm)/Pt at 25°C. All other electrodes measured relative to this reference (0V)

questions.question: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode. Zn/Cu cell (E°Zn=-0.76V, E°Cu=+0.34V). E°cell=
questions.explanation: E°cell=0.34-(-0.76)=1.10V (positive=spontaneous)

questions.question: More positive electrode potential means
questions.explanation: Higher E°: species more easily reduced → stronger oxidising agent (e.g. F₂ with highest E° is strongest oxidant)

questions.question: Cathode in electrochemical cell is where
questions.explanation: Cathode: reduction (gain electrons). Anode: oxidation (lose electrons). Mnemonic: OILRIG, Red Cat An Ox

questions.question: ΔG = -nFE°. If E°cell is positive, ΔG is
questions.explanation: Positive E°cell → negative ΔG → spontaneous reaction. Consistent thermodynamics

questions.question: Salt bridge in galvanic cell
options: maintains electrical neutrality by allowing ion flow between half-cells
questions.answer: maintains electrical neutrality by allowing ion flow between half-cells
questions.explanation: Salt bridge: allows slow ion migration to balance charge accumulation in each half-cell without mixing solutions

questions.question: Lead-acid battery in a car is rechargeable because
options: the electrochemical reactions are reversible — driving current reverses the reactions
questions.answer: the electrochemical reactions are reversible — driving current reverses the reactions
questions.explanation: Rechargeable batteries: applying external voltage reverses the discharge reactions, restoring reactants

questions.question: Nernst equation shows cell potential
questions.explanation: E=E°-(RT/nF)lnQ: as concentrations change (Q changes), cell potential deviates from E°

questions.question: Hydrogen fuel cell produces
questions.explanation: H₂+½O₂→H₂O. Only product is water — zero emission during operation

questions.question: Standard conditions for measuring electrode potentials include
options: 25°C, all ions at 1M concentration, gases at 1atm pressure
questions.answer: 25°C, all ions at 1M concentration, gases at 1atm pressure
questions.explanation: Standard: T=298K(25°C), concentration=1mol/L, gas pressure=1atm (101.325kPa)

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Equilibria. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Equilibria concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Equilibria builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Equilibria. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Equilibria. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Equilibria include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Equilibria. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Equilibria. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Equilibria concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Equilibria builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Equilibria. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Equilibria. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Equilibria include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Equilibria. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
